---
title: "DP as Hypothesis Testing"
subtitle: "Applied Differential Privacy"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: white
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    scrollable: true
    css: slides.css
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| include: false
# Environment setup: pick up local edits to libdpy/ without restarting the kernel,
# and install the public library when missing (e.g. on Google Colab).
# autoreload is only a local-dev convenience (live-reloading libdpy edits). Skip it if the
# extension can't load - e.g. on Colab's Python 3.12, where IPython's autoreload still imports
# the removed `imp` module and raises ModuleNotFoundError.
try:
    ip = get_ipython()
    if "autoreload" not in ip.extension_manager.loaded:
        ip.run_line_magic("load_ext", "autoreload")
        ip.run_line_magic("autoreload", "2")
except Exception:
    pass

try:
    import libdpy
except ImportError:
    %pip install -q "libdpy[notebook] @ git+https://github.com/applied-dp-course/pub_lib.git@v0.2.19"
    import libdpy

# Register inline figures for Quarto/RevealJS export.
%matplotlib inline
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| include: false
from libdpy.visualization.privacy_plots import PrivacyPlot
from libdpy.visualization.roc_plots import make_roc_decision_declarative_figure
from libdpy.assignment_specific.dp_as_hypothesis_testing.utils import desicion_rule_params
from libdpy.assignment_specific.dp_as_hypothesis_testing.lecture_figures import (
    make_analytic_roc_curve_figure,
    make_analytic_roc_curves_by_std_figure,
    make_empty_roc_plane_figure,
    make_fpr_tpr_confusion_panels_figure,
    make_hypothesis_distributions_figure,
    make_threshold_decision_rule_figure,
)
from scipy.stats import norm, laplace, uniform
```

## Recap: privacy and anonymity

- Multiple anonymization / privacy-preserving heuristics were proposed — **all of them were breached at some point.**
- Reconstruction attacks provide a *systematic* way to de-anonymize data.
- There is a need for a **rigorous mathematical definition** of privacy.
- To do so, let's take the **adversary's** perspective.

## Modeling the adversary

The strongest possible adversary knows **the inner workings of the mechanism** and considers only **two possible datasets**:
one *with* the target user, and one *without* them.

> **How can we model the attacker's task in this setting?**


## Modeling the adversary

The strongest possible adversary knows **the inner workings of the mechanism** and considers only **two possible datasets**:
one *with* the target user, and one *without* them.

> **How can we model the attacker's task in this setting?**

Distinguishing between two distributions based on a single draw from one of them. 

In the statistical literature this is referred to as *hypothesis testing*, and in the CS community - *binary classification*.


## Hypothesis testing and binary classification

### Two distributions

Example: $\mathbf{H_0}$: $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma^2)$ (negative) and $\mathbf{H_1}$: $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma^2)$ (positive).

```{python}
dist_neg = norm(0, 1)
dist_pos = norm(1, 1)
make_hypothesis_distributions_figure(dist_neg, dist_pos)
```


## Hypothesis testing and binary classification

### Confusion matrix

<div class="confusion-wrap confusion-stage-1">
<div class="confusion-layout">
<div class="confusion-corner"></div>
<div class="confusion-col-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Actual</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Guess</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TN</abbr><span>True negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FN</abbr><span>False negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FP</abbr><span>False positive</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TP</abbr><span>True positive</span></div></div>
</div>
<div class="confusion-caption-slot">
<p class="confusion-caption">Columns correspond to the <strong>actual</strong> class; rows correspond to the rule's <strong>guess</strong>.</p>
</div>
</div>

## Decision rule

A function from the domain to $\{0, 1\}$, which given a sampled value guesses whether it was sampled from $H_0$ or $H_1$.

```{python}
tau = 0.35
make_threshold_decision_rule_figure(dist_neg, dist_pos, tau)
```


## Confusion matrix

<div class="confusion-wrap confusion-stage-2">
<div class="confusion-layout">
<div class="confusion-corner"></div>
<div class="confusion-col-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Actual</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Guess</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TN</abbr><span>True negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FN</abbr><span>False negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FP</abbr><span>False positive</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TP</abbr><span>True positive</span></div></div>
</div>
<div class="confusion-caption-slot">
<p class="confusion-caption">Columns correspond to the <strong>actual</strong> class; rows correspond to the rule's <strong>guess</strong>.</p>
</div>
</div>


## Confusion matrix

<div class="confusion-wrap confusion-stage-3">
<div class="confusion-layout">
<div class="confusion-corner"></div>
<div class="confusion-col-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Actual</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-col-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-axis"><span class="confusion-label">Guess</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Negative</span></div>
<div class="confusion-row-label"><span class="confusion-label">Positive</span></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TN</abbr><span>True negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FN</abbr><span>False negative</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>FP</abbr><span>False positive</span></div></div>
<div class="confusion-cell"><div class="confusion-cell-content"><abbr>TP</abbr><span>True positive</span></div></div>
</div>
<div class="confusion-caption-slot">
<p class="confusion-caption">Columns correspond to the <strong>actual</strong> class; rows correspond to the rule's <strong>guess</strong>.</p>
</div>
</div>

## FP and TP

```{python}
make_fpr_tpr_confusion_panels_figure(dist_neg, dist_pos, tau)
```

## FPR and TPR

- The probability that an element drawn from $H_1$ is marked positive is the **True Positive Rate (TPR)**.
- The probability that an element drawn from $H_0$ is marked positive is the **False Positive Rate (FPR)**.

For a threshold decision rule:
$$FPR := \Pr_{X \sim H_0}(X > \tau), \qquad TPR := \Pr_{X \sim H_1}(X > \tau).$$

## The ROC plane

Naturally, there is a tradeoff between TPR and FPR. This tradeoff is represented by the **ROC plane**. Any decision rule is a point with coordinates $(FPR, TPR)$.

```{python}
make_empty_roc_plane_figure(title='ROC plane', square=True)
```

## The ROC curve

For any FPR there is a maximal achievable TPR; the collection of these points is the **ROC curve**.

```{python}
make_analytic_roc_curve_figure(dist_neg, dist_pos, square=True)
```


## The ROC curve

Of course, this curve depends on $\mu_1 - \mu_0$ and $\sigma$.

```{python}
make_analytic_roc_curves_by_std_figure([0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5], square=True)
```

## Case study: two Binomials

Consider two Binomial random variables: $H_0 = \mathrm{Bin}(0.5, 5)$ and $H_1 = \mathrm{Bin}(0.25, 5)$.

- Compute the ratio $P(i)/Q(i)$ for $i \in [5]$.
- What are all the possible decision rules?
- Compute the FPR and TPR for all possible decisions.
- Draw the ROC curve.

## Case study: two Binomials

Consider two Binomial random variables: $H_0 = \mathrm{Bin}(0.5, 5)$ and $H_1 = \mathrm{Bin}(0.25, 5)$.

```{python}
# Start from an empty ROC plane and fill it in together.
make_empty_roc_plane_figure(title='ROC plane')
```

## Connecting the threshold to the ROC
### Gaussian distributions

Sweeping the threshold traces a curve in the ROC plane.

```{python}
make_roc_decision_declarative_figure(desicion_rule_params, dist_neg, dist_pos, True)
```

## Connecting the threshold to the ROC
### Uniform distributions

```{python}
make_roc_decision_declarative_figure(desicion_rule_params, uniform(0, 3), uniform(1, 3), True)
```

## Neyman–Pearson lemma

Every optimal decision rule is a threshold on the log probability ratio.
If the log probability ratio is monotonic in $x$, a threshold on the value itself is optimal.

```{python}
make_roc_decision_declarative_figure(desicion_rule_params, dist_neg, dist_pos, False)
```

## Privacy as a bound on the ROC curve

We model the privacy threat as the attacker trying to distinguish two hypotheses: $H_0$ — the user was **not** in the dataset — and $H_1$ — the user **was** in the dataset. The two distributions represent the mechanism's output distribution given each dataset.

The closer the ROC curve is to the diagonal, the harder it is for the attacker to succeed — so a natural privacy definition **upper-bounds this curve**. One simple option is a linear bound: $TPR \le m \cdot FPR + n$, where $m$ is the slope and $n$ the shift (plus its reflected part for the top half).

## Privacy as a bound on the ROC curve

```{python}
PrivacyPlot(
    sensitivity=1,
    std=1.5,
    distribution_types=[norm],
).embed(mode="deck")
```

## Is this a new privacy definition?

Did we just develop a new (and more natural) privacy definition than differential privacy?

Apparently not. The tradeoff function is bounded by the lines $TPR \le m \cdot FPR + n$ **iff** it is $(\ln m, n)$-DP. Isn't that a miracle?

## Is this a new privacy definition?

Let's use this insight to reason about the privacy of several other noise-addition mechanisms:

```{python}
PrivacyPlot(
    sensitivity=1,
    std=1.5,
    distribution_types=[norm, laplace, uniform],
).embed(mode="deck")
```

## Summary

- Membership-inference attack models the **strongest** adversary.
- The adversary's task is essentially **binary classification**.
- Binary classification is analyzed using the **ROC curve**.
- Optimal decision rules are **probability-ratio thresholds** (Neyman–Pearson).
- **Differential privacy upper-bounds** the probability ratio — and hence the ROC curve.
